results.title: [PDF] A Deterministic Control Plane for LLM Coding Agents - arXiv
results.content:
Instead: The configuration-and-process layer that surrounds an LLM coding agent can be treated as a managed software supply chain, governed deterministically and independently of the underlying harness or model, addressing critical in-tegrity, traceability, and portability risks that the harness alone does not.
The word deterministic is load-bearing. Every governance mechanism we pro-pose is implemented in ordinary, testable code (hashing, regex matching, a state machine, set arithmetic), not delegated to an LLM. A non-deterministic component cannot serve as a trustworthy control for another non-deterministic component. [...] This creates a determinism boundary central to the architecture’s honesty: trace linkage depends on the agent’s cooperative invocation of the trace tool. A 2Phase numbering (0, 1, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8) is non-contiguous by design: phases 2 and 6 are reserved for future specialist sub-workflows without renumbering deployed systems.
20 misconfigured, prompt-injected, or non-compliant agent that edits files without calling trace-update will have its changes surface as scope-creep warnings at the next drift check - detectable but not preventable before the edit lands.
In a threat model where the same LLM can be steered by injected instructions, relying on that agent to faithfully call trace-update is an honor-system com-ponent inside an otherwise deterministic control plane. [...] end_phase(n): if not metrics[n].start_ts: fail END_WITHOUT_START if required_delegations not ⊆recorded[n]: fail DELEGATION_GATE if n==0 and tier∈{M,L} and 'spec-reviewer' ∉recorded: fail REVIEW_GATE if delegations[n] ≠∅and securityScan[n].status ≠clean: fail SECURITY_SCAN_GATE commit(end_ts, active = total −hitl_pause) Because these checks are hard-coded in the control plane, they cannot be by-passed via persuasive LLM prompting.

results.title: A Deterministic Control Plane for LLM Coding Agents - arXiv
results.content:
•Authoring & Distribution Plane - A canonical registry of agents, skills, knowledge shards, profiles, and workflows, secured with content hashes. A CLI and an MCP server expose install, search, compose, and governance operations. 
   •Compilation Plane - A transformer that normalises a canonical Markdown+YAML definition and emits IDE-native files for diverse targets, explicitly ordering static content first to optimize for prompt-cache efficiency. 
   •Runtime-Governance Plane - Deterministic helpers (CLI scripts and/or MCP tools) that the in-IDE agent calls to advance phase state, record delegations, and update the requirement→file→test trace. This plane enforces permissions and command/path blocklists at install-time and pre-tool-use. 

![Image 3: [Uncaptioned image]]( [...] This creates a determinism boundary central to the architecture’s honesty: trace linkage depends on the agent’s cooperative invocation of the trace tool. A misconfigured, prompt-injected, or non-compliant agent that edits files without calling trace-update will have its changes surface as scope-creep warnings at the next drift check - detectable but not preventable before the edit lands. In a threat model where the same LLM can be steered by injected instructions, relying on that agent to faithfully call trace-update is an honor-system component inside an otherwise deterministic control plane. The control plane’s response is auditability and detection, not pre-execution file-scope enforcement. A DRIFT-detection vignette (Appendix D) shows the dominant trace gap is _implementation without [...] > The configuration-and-process layer that surrounds an LLM coding agent can be treated as a managed software supply chain, governed _deterministically_ and _independently of the underlying harness or model_, addressing critical integrity, traceability, and portability risks that the harness alone does not.

The word _deterministic_ is load-bearing. Every governance mechanism we propose is implemented in ordinary, testable code (hashing, regex matching, a state machine, set arithmetic), not delegated to an LLM. A non-deterministic component cannot serve as a trustworthy control for another non-deterministic component.

### 1.3 Contributions

We propose a control plane architecture and detail its reference implementation, offering five architectural contributions:
